prompt: Explain the water cycle, in prose, without using any lists, bullets, headings, or numbered steps. Keep it to one paragraph.
response: Water moves through a continuous journey across our planet in a process we call the water cycle, which begins when the sun heats up water from oceans, lakes, and rivers, transforming it into water vapor that rises into the atmosphere. This invisible moisture cools at higher altitudes and condenses into tiny water droplets to form clouds, a process we witness as precipitation when these droplets grow heavy and fall back to Earth as rain, snow, or hail, replenishing water bodies and soaking into the ground. Once on the surface, some of this water flows over land as runoff, making its way back to larger bodies of water, while some infiltrates into the soil and becomes groundwater, continuing its subterranean journey to eventually emerge in springs or seep into surface waters. Throughout this circular path, water changes forms repeatedly—from liquid to gas to ice—moving constantly between the atmosphere, land, and water bodies without beginning or end, sustaining all life on Earth through this endless, mysterious dance of moisture that connects all corners of our planet in an unbroken cycle.
